skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec.
skill.summary: Programmatic video rendering from React/Remotion TSX components via the inference.sh CLI.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: The skill wraps the inference.sh belt CLI to render Remotion TSX components to MP4 video. Static analysis flagged 32 external command locations — all are legitimate `belt app run infsh/remotion-render` invocations properly scoped by `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)` in the frontmatter. The 6 network references point to the legitimate inference.sh service and documentation. The 3 weak-crypto findings are false positives triggered by the word 'codec' and import patterns, not actual cryptographic code. No prompt injection or malicious intent was detected.

low_findings.title: External service dependency
low_findings.description: The skill requires the inference.sh belt CLI and sends user-provided TSX code to an external rendering service. While the service is legitimate and the tool scope is restricted to `Bash(belt *)`, users should be aware that code is transmitted to a third-party endpoint for rendering.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network calls to inference.sh are expected for a rendering service, but represent a data transmission to an external third party that users should be informed about.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell command execution via belt CLI (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis flagged 32 locations of backtick/shell execution patterns. All occurrences are legitimate `belt app run infsh/remotion-render` CLI invocations in code examples and documentation. The frontmatter restricts permissions to `Bash(belt *)`, limiting execution surface. No command injection risk identified.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All detected patterns are belt CLI invocations with hardcoded arguments, scoped by allowed-tools. No user-controlled input flows into shell execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded service URLs (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis flagged 6 hardcoded URLs. All are legitimate references to inference.sh service endpoints and documentation. No suspicious or untrusted domains detected.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to the documented inference.sh service. No data exfiltration to unknown endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis flagged weak crypto patterns at lines 3, 3, and 35. These are false positives — line 3 contains the skill description mentioning 'codec' and line 35 is the parameter table header. No actual cryptographic operations exist in the file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: No crypto code present; triggers likely from the word 'codec' and table formatting. Verified by full file read.

content.user_title: Render Remotion Videos from TSX Code
content.value_statement: Programmatic video creation requires complex tooling and infrastructure. This skill enables generating MP4 videos from React/Remotion component code using the inference.sh CLI.
actual_capabilities: Render React/Remotion TSX components to MP4 video via the belt CLI
Support all major Remotion APIs including useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, and Sequence
Configure output resolution, FPS, duration, and codec parameters
Pass dynamic props to components for data-driven video generation
Stream rendering progress and retrieve output URLs in real time
Integrate with the Python SDK for automated video generation pipelines
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and an active account
Limited to Remotion and React imports — no arbitrary npm packages
Output format is constrained to codecs supported by the rendering service
Rendering time depends on the inference.sh platform's queue and processing capacity

use_cases.title: Automated social media content
use_cases.description: Generate branded short-form videos from templates and dynamic data for marketing teams.

use_cases.title: Data-driven video reports
use_cases.description: Create animated charts and data visualizations rendered as video for dashboards and presentations.

use_cases.title: Programmatic video pipelines
use_cases.description: Integrate Remotion rendering into CI/CD or batch processing workflows using the Python SDK.

prompt_templates.title: Simple text animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 3-second 1080p video with white text fading in on a black background using Remotion and the belt CLI.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with a basic fade-in text animation

prompt_templates.title: Animated counter with props
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Remotion video that counts from 0 to 100% over 5 seconds at 60fps, with a loading label below the counter. Pass the label as a prop.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a progress indicator with dynamic props

prompt_templates.title: Spring-based motion design
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 2-second 1080p video featuring a white card that scales in with a spring animation (damping: 10, stiffness: 100) on a purple background.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating motion graphics with physics-based spring animations

prompt_templates.title: Multi-sequence video with Python SDK
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Python script using the inference.sh SDK to render a 4-second video with three sequential text labels ('First', 'Second', 'Third') fading in at 0s, 1s, and 2s.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced pipeline integration with streaming progress

output_examples.input: Request: Render a 3-second video with 'Hello World' fading in over 1 second on a black background.
output_examples.output: A 1920x1080 MP4 file at 30fps, 3 seconds long, with white text fading from 0% to 100% opacity over the first 30 frames.

output_examples.input: Request: Create a 5-second progress counter from 0% to 100% at 60fps with a loading label.
output_examples.output: A 1080x1080 MP4 at 60fps showing a percentage counter incrementing from 0 to 100 over 5 seconds, with a 'Loading...' label below, returned with a streaming progress callback.

best_practices: Always export a default function from your TSX component for the renderer to pick it up
Use AbsoluteFill as the root container to ensure full-canvas coverage across all resolutions
Leverage useVideoConfig for FPS and duration calculations instead of hardcoding values
anti_patterns: Hardcoding FPS or duration values in your component instead of reading from useVideoConfig
Using fixed pixel dimensions rather than relative units for layout scaling
Importing external npm packages not supported by the Remotion render environment

faq.question: What CLI tool does this skill use?
faq.answer: This skill uses the belt CLI from inference.sh. Install it with the provided instructions and run `belt login` before rendering.

faq.question: Can I use arbitrary npm packages in my Remotion code?
faq.answer: No. The render environment only supports imports from 'remotion' and 'react'. External npm packages are not available.

faq.question: How do I control video resolution and frame rate?
faq.answer: Pass 'width', 'height', 'fps', and 'duration_seconds' parameters in the input JSON to the belt command.

faq.question: Is there a Python SDK available?
faq.answer: Yes. The inference.sh Python SDK supports both synchronous and streaming modes for rendering Remotion videos programmatically.

faq.question: What output format is produced?
faq.answer: The renderer outputs an MP4 video file. The codec can be configured via the 'codec' input parameter.

faq.question: Can I pass dynamic data to my component?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the 'props' parameter in the input JSON to pass any serializable object to your component's default export.
